title: Deterministic Nucleation and Dynamics of Infilled Multiply-Charged Vortices in an Immiscible $^{87}\mathrm{Rb}$-$^{41}\mathrm{K}$ Mixture
abstract: We propose a method for controllably generating multiply-charged vortices in immiscible Bose-Einstein condensates. We achieve this by applying a laser stirring technique to a $^{87}\mathrm{Rb}$-$^{41}\mathrm{K}$ mixture, where the vortices generated are infilled by the secondary component. We numerically demonstrate that the charge of the vortex can be tuned reproducibly by varying the stirring parameters, allowing the deterministic generation of stable infilled vortices with high topological charge. We then consider the dynamics of these multiply-charged vortices in a circular trap; in contrast to single-component condensates, we observe long-lived precession of the multiply-charged vortices with a charge dependent frequency and collective breathing modes of the infilling component. For sufficiently large winding numbers, we observe distinct dynamical instabilities leading to vortex dislocation.
summary: This paper presents a method to controllably create multiply-charged vortices in a mixture of 87Rb and 41K Bose-Einstein condensates. By using a spiral laser stirring technique, the researchers show they can deterministically set the vortex charge. They find that the second component fills the vortex cores, which helps stabilize high-charge vortices against splitting. The study also characterizes the resulting dynamics, including precession and shape oscillations, and identifies specific ways these vortices eventually become unstable.
main_problem: Developing a method for the deterministic generation and studying the stability and dynamics of multiply-charged vortices in an immiscible binary Bose-Einstein condensate.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a spiral stirring technique can reproducibly tune vortex charge and that the presence of an infilling component can stabilize high-charge vortices, though distinct dynamical instabilities (core elongation and node development) occur at high winding numbers.
method: Numerical evolution of coupled 2D Gross-Pitaevskii equations using the xmds2 code with an adaptive Runge-Kutta method, supplemented by a massive point-vortex model for predicting precession.
model_or_system: An immiscible mixture of 87Rb and 41K atoms in a quasi-2D, hard-walled circular trap, modeled by coupled Gross-Pitaevskii equations including intra- and inter-species interaction strengths.
key_observables: Vortex winding number (charge q), density profiles of both components, phase winding, precession frequency, and the aspect ratio (shape oscillations) of the infilling component.
important_parameters: Mass ratio (approx 2.12), interaction strength ratios (alpha and beta), vortex charge (q), stirring parameters (beam width, number of spirals, and stirring time), and atom number ratio.
main_assumptions: Zero-temperature limit and a quasi-2D approximation via strong harmonic trapping in the z-direction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the evolution of density and phase during the spiral stirring process; Figure 2 relates phase winding to stirring parameters; Figure 4 shows vortex precession and its linear dependence on charge; Figure 5 shows the temporal evolution and frequency of infilling component shape oscillations; Figure 6 illustrates the mechanism of vortex dislocation for high charges.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of vortex generation, describes the numerical setup and stirring technique, presents results on deterministic charge tuning, analyzes the stability and dynamics (precession and breathing modes) of the vortices, and concludes with an investigation of specific instability mechanisms.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and AMO physics as it explores how a second species can be used to control and stabilize topological defects, providing a platform for studying complex superfluid dynamics and symmetry-breaking instabilities.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Stills from a simulation of the spiral technique with Ns = 3 and Ts = 104, which results in an infilled vortex with charge q = 8 at the origin. Here the beam width w = 32, the trap radius R = 100, and N2 = 1000. The first column shows the density of component 1, |ψ1|2; the second column shows the phase of component 1, Arg (ψ1); the third column shows the density of component 2, |ψ2|2. For clarity, we only plot the phase of component 1 where |ψ1|2 > 0.01, and the trajectory of the spiral beam is added in white to the third column as a guide. The rows correspond to different times, (a) at t = 0, (b) at t = 2 × 103, (c) at t = 4 × 103, (d) at t = 6 × 103, (e) at t = 8 × 103, (f) at t =...

FIG. 2. The resulting phase winding pinned at the origin as a function of TR and Ns for (a) w = 16 and (b) w = 32. Black crosses (x) indicate values of Ts and Ns where the stirrer moved faster than the critical velocity for vortex nucleation and additional vortices were shed.

FIG. 3. Snapshots of the vortex created at the origin as the obstacle potential is ramped off. The top panel shows a schematic of the ramp-off protocol. Rows (a)-(d) show the density of the main component, |ψ1|2, row (e) shows the phase of the main component, Arg (ψ1). Snapshots in a given row are taken at the same time, the time of each row corresponds to text in the top panel. As before, we only plot the phase of component 1 where |ψ1|2 > 0.01. As a guide to the eye, the black density contour |ψ1 (THold) |2 = 0.01 is plotted for each set of stirring parameters. The white contour displays |ψ2(t)|2 = 0.5. The columns correspond to different stirring parameters: Column (1), w = 16, Ns = 3,...

FIG. 4. Main panel: The precession of the vortex, ϕv, as a function of time. The charge of the vortex, q, is indicated; the infilling atom number, N2, and the interaction parameters, α and β, are all fixed. Upper panel: The extracted precession frequency of the vortex, ˙ϕv, for each of the vortex charges; the green line shows the least squares fit ˙ϕv = Aq + B with A = 1.279 × 10−4 and B = 1.705 × 10−5.

FIG. 5. Main panel: The aspect ratio of the infilling compo- nent, given by Eq. (20), as a function of time. The charge of the vortex, q is indicated on the vertical axis, the colourbar represents λ. Upper panel: The extracted frequency of the as- pect ratio, fλ, as a function of vortex charge, found by fitting Eq. (21); the inset shows the coefficient of determination for each value of q. Note we only present extracted frequencies where the coefficient of determination is greater than 0.75, indicated by the solid red line.

FIG. 6. Snapshots of the phase of the off-center vortex as it precesses around the trap. Columns correspond to different vortex charges: Column 1, q=7 (unstable); Column 2, q=10 (stable); Column 3, q = 13 (unstable). As a guide to the eye, dislocated vortices are highlighted with a red circle, and the density contours |ψ2|2 = {0.1, 0.5} are plotted in black. Rows show the temporal evolution of the phase at (a) t = 103, (b) t = 4×103, (c) t = 7×103, (d) t = 1.05×104, (e) t = 1.25×104, and (f) t = 1.5 × 104. The bottom right inset is a zoomed in view of panel (f3). A movie of this simulation is available in the Supplemental Material [61].
